TASBY program class of 2025 and officially designate her a Master Trustee, a distinction earned by
fewer than a thousand school board trustees across the state of Texas since the program's founding.
Before I speak to you about her accomplishment, I'd like to offer a brief moment of reflection on
TASB's own story because it mirrors the kind of leadership Don represents. As you may or may not
know, TASB was formed more than 80 years ago in the midst of World War II when military bases were
being rapidly built across the state of Texas. Almost overnight new school districts were spring.

[02:30–03:00]
School districts were springing up to serve children of servicemen and women. The Texas Education
Agency simply could not keep up with the pace of that growth, so in true Texas fashion, local school
boards stepped up. They came together and formed a nonprofit cooperative to support one another,
share resources, and advocate for strong local governance. Today, Tasby is governed by 43 locally
elected school board trustees, one of which is Master Trustee Director Rebecca Fox, thank you for
your service. Um, that quarterly meet to set policies that guide our work and help every

[03:00–03:30]
Texas school board, regardless of size, geography, or budget, access the tools they need to serve
students well. TASB here to save districts' money and to fight on behalf of 5.5 million Texas public
school children. And that legacy of selfless local leadership is exactly the spirit Don Champaign
has brought to her leadership TASBE journey. Over the past year, Master Trustee Champagne traveled
more than 2,300 miles across the state as a part of the class of 2025. 34 other school board
trustees across the state, beginning in San Antonio.

The statute clearly relieves the teacher of any duty during this period of time and prohibits the
district and its administration from requiring the teacher to engage in any other activity the
administration determines to be useful and important. Dr. Grigorski, last week you stated that your
position has always been to follow the Texas Education Code, which is great. But you suggested that
if teachers had complaints, we should take them up with our chain of command. Principals, area
superintendents, or you directly, which I trust would be effective if it were affecting just a few
teachers in campuses.

[18:30–19:00]
However, since the start of this 24-25 school year, 171 teachers from 56 KDISD campuses have
reported regularly losing at least one planning period a week. This is 78% of KDISD elementary
schools, 61% of KD junior highs, and 80% of KD high schools for a total of 74% of campuses across
the district that have teachers regularly losing state protected planning time.

[19:00–19:30]
And this is just from a short survey. Imagine what the actual number is. These numbers are alarming
and clearly not a coincidence or a one off, nor is it a problem isolated to individuals in any way.
This issue originates at the district level. The mandate for these planning periods was given
without considering the legal implications, and now the responsibility is unfairly being swept down
to campus administrators to fix a compliance issue created by district administration. We've raised
this concern multiple times with district administrators, including directly to area
superintendents, yet the response has been silence, dismissal, or even
